name: Cindy Holscher
occupation: Kansas State Senator and former business professional
education: University of Missouri (Journalism, Marketing, and Political Science); first college graduate in her family
family: Husband Greg; three children, all graduates of Kansas public schools
whoTheyAre:
Cindy Holscher is a Democratic state senator from Overland Park running for governor of Kansas. She was born in Slater, Missouri, the daughter of a union construction worker and an elementary school custodian. Her grandparents were tenant farmers. She was the first person in her family to graduate from college, earning degrees in journalism, marketing, and political science from the University of Missouri. She has lived in Kansas with her husband Greg and their three children for more than 25 years.

Before entering politics, Holscher spent over a decade at Sprint, where she developed and oversaw multi-million-dollar budgets and won awards for employee development. She also worked at R.H. Donnelley and The Add Sheet earlier in her career. Her entry into public life was driven by education advocacy. She says she walked with a group of education advocates from Shawnee to Topeka twice to push for better school funding during the Brownback era.

Holscher won her first race in 2016, defeating Republican incumbent Amanda Grosserode for a Kansas House seat in Johnson County. She moved to the state Senate in 2020 by defeating former Republican representative James Todd. She won re-election to the Senate in 2024 by a significant margin. She co-founded the Women's Bipartisan Caucus in the Kansas Legislature and has built her public identity around working-class roots, family economics, and public school advocacy.
theirRecord:
Holscher has served nine years in the Kansas Legislature, four in the House and five in the Senate. Her most prominent legislative work has centered on public school funding, Medicaid expansion, and opposition to school vouchers. She was part of the bipartisan coalition that reversed the Brownback-era tax cuts in 2017, which she treats as a defining moment in her political career. She fought to expand Medicaid eligibility in Kansas, backing a bill that passed the House but fell short of overcoming the governor's veto. She has been one of the loudest Democratic voices against school voucher proposals, arguing that 60 Kansas counties have no private schools and that vouchers amount to a transfer of public money to families who already send their children to private institutions.

On the other side of the ledger, Holscher is running for governor as a clear underdog within her own party. Governor Laura Kelly endorsed her primary opponent, Ethan Corson, and Holscher publicly accused the Kelly administration of pressuring Democratic senators not to run so that the field could be cleared for a preferred candidate. She told the Kansas Reflector in August 2025 that she and others were told to stand aside, saying she felt 'bullied' by the process. Her fundraising has been significantly lower than Corson's, with roughly $397,000 raised compared to his $902,000 through the 2025 year-end reporting period.

Her legislative record is more extensive than most candidates in the Democratic field, but critics point out that she represents a safe Johnson County seat and has limited reach in the rural parts of the state where Democrats need to gain ground. She has taken a more confrontational posture toward the Republican supermajority than many Kansas Democrats, which appeals to her base but may complicate a general election pitch in a deeply red state.
whatYouShouldKnow: Holscher is a sitting Kansas state senator with nine years of legislative experience, giving voters a real voting record to evaluate.
She co-founded the Women's Bipartisan Caucus in the Kansas Legislature, one of the clearest examples of cross-party work in her career.
She was part of the bipartisan coalition that reversed the Brownback-era tax cuts in 2017, a pivotal moment in Kansas fiscal policy.

She has been one of the most vocal opponents of school vouchers in the state, arguing that 60 Kansas counties have no private schools and that vouchers primarily benefit families who are already wealthy.

Governor Laura Kelly's staff reportedly pressured Holscher and other Democratic senators not to run for governor so the field could be cleared for a preferred candidate. Holscher went public with the accusation, calling it bullying.
Her 2025 year-end finance report shows $397,952 raised, $218,060 spent, and $179,892 cash on hand, putting her well behind Ethan Corson's $902,641 in total fundraising.
She supports Medicaid expansion, reproductive rights, and the legalization of medical marijuana.

No public church affiliation was found in any campaign materials, news coverage, or social media profiles reviewed. She attended events at a Unitarian Universalist church and an Episcopal church, but those appear to have been campaign stops rather than personal worship.
whereTheyWorship:
No public church affiliation, denomination, or faith statement could be confirmed for Cindy Holscher in the materials reviewed. Her campaign website, social media profiles, and news coverage do not reference a personal faith or church membership. She attended a meeting at Shawnee Mission Unitarian Universalist Church in Lenexa in October 2025 and held a campaign event at St. Andrew's Episcopal Church parish hall in Emporia, but both appear to have been campaign events held at church facilities rather than indicators of personal membership.

In a Democratic primary in Kansas, candidates are less likely to lead with faith credentials. However, the complete absence of any faith language in her public profile is a data point worth noting for voters who consider that important.

quotes.text: The push for 'school choice' is just another way to get more of our taxpayer dollars into the hands of those who are already wealthy.
Politicians are making dangerous cuts to our programs, Medicaid and Social Security. That's hurting us, and with the expiration of ACA tax credits, that's a huge threat.
As governor, I will push back and pull every lever out there to protect our people and to bring health care and what we need across the state.
All three of us were told basically 'do not run' because Chris Mann's the guy.
Why are insiders picking candidates? We're being bullied, essentially.
I walked with a group of education advocates from Shawnee to Topeka twice.
School vouchers are a scam, a transfer of wealth to the already rich. About 70% of vouchers go to rich families already sending their kids to private school.

campaignFinance.narrative: Holscher's 2025 year-end report shows a modest but steady fundraising operation. She raised $397,952.07 and spent $218,060.34, leaving $179,891.73 cash on hand. The campaign is not self-funded; there are no personal loans on the filing. Her donor base appears to be built on regular contributions from individual supporters rather than a few large checks. She is significantly behind her primary opponent Ethan Corson, who raised $902,641 in the same period. The gap in resources reflects the broader challenge Holscher faces as the non-establishment candidate in the Democratic primary.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The full donor schedule was not completely parsed. Additional contributors including Ross Boelling appear later in the filing. The early itemized entries suggest a broad base of small-to-mid-sized individual donors.
